summary: DeVault outlines his philosophy for using IRC productively, arguing that instant messaging should be treated as ephemeral and asynchronous. He explains that users should never feel obligated to catch up on missed discussions, and any actionable information must be moved to a more permanent medium like email or bug trackers. He advocates pulling notifications rather than having them pushed, including full context in mentions, and dumping all relevant information upfront rather than engaging in slow back-and-forth dialogue. The approach eliminates the stress and productivity loss that many people associate with chat tools.
key_points: Instant messaging is ephemeral: important information must be moved to permanent media like mailing lists, bug trackers, or wikis
Requiring issues to leave IRC before becoming actionable filters out invalid or duplicate tasks and improves signal-to-noise ratio
IM should be treated as asynchronous — dump full context upfront rather than expecting real-time dialogue
Pull notifications instead of having them pushed: glance at the client when convenient rather than enabling desktop alerts
Always include enough context in mentions so recipients can understand without reading backlog
money_quotes: The most important trait to consider when using IM software is that it is ephemeral, and must be treated as such.
If the task never leaves IRC, it also never gets done.

Many invalid or duplicate tasks are filtered out by this approach, and those which do get mode-shifted often have more detail than they otherwise might, which improves the signal-to-noise ratio on my bug trackers and mailing lists.
I 'pull' notifications from it instead of having it 'push' them to me — that is, I glance at the client every now and then.
You should not 'catch up' on discussions that you missed, and should not expect others to do so, either.
Many, many companies have tried, and failed, to re-invent IRC, usually within a proprietary walled garden.
key_insight: Instant messaging becomes productive only when treated as ephemeral and asynchronous, with all actionable information deliberately moved to more permanent media.
